---
title: "Why measure transferability risk?"
description: >
  Complete certification of a safety filter is impossible, so the right goal is
  measurement. This opens a series on porting the Cox & Bunzel method to text
  safety classifiers.
date: "2026-06-16"
categories: [ai-security, adversarial-ml]
draft: false
---

This is the first post in a series that builds, in the open, a tool to measure how
easily a text safety classifier can be fooled by adversarial examples crafted on
*other* models.

## The starting point

Safety classifiers — prompt-injection detectors, jailbreak filters, content
gates — are deployed as if they were guarantees. They are not. Vassilev (2025)
gives the clean negative result: the set of adversarial prompts that evade any
finite checker is infinite, so no classifier can ever cover it. Asking "is this
filter safe?" has no useful answer.

A better question is comparative: *how leaky is this filter, relative to others,
and which other models can an attacker use to find prompts that fool it?* Cox &
Bunzel (2025) make that question operational in the image domain. They select
surrogate models that bracket a target in representational similarity (measured by
Centered Kernel Alignment), attack the surrogates, measure how often the attacks
transfer to the target, and fit a regression that predicts transfer rate from
similarity. This series ports that method to text safety classifiers, starting
with a prompt-injection detector.

## What the series will cover

- The method and the measurement-not-certification boundary (this post).
- Representational similarity: CKA and Diagonal Box Similarity, with the
  invariance tests that keep the implementation honest.
- The surrogate pool, fine-tuning, and the deliberate non-transformer outlier.
- Attacks and transfer: TextAttack recipes against surrogates, then the frozen
  target.
- The headline result: does CKA-guided surrogate selection beat random selection?

## Reproducibility

Every post corresponds to runnable code in the
[repository](https://github.com/turn1a/safety-classifier-transfer-risk).
Pipelines are built with Kedro, runs are tracked in MLflow, and the environment is
pinned with uv. The intent is that a reader can clone the repo and reproduce the
numbers, not just read about them.

*The pipeline code referenced here is currently a scaffold; the posts will fill in
as each phase lands.*
